questions.question: Reference the previous question. If your planned fuel flow is 9 gph and your destination is 71 nm away, how much fuel will you need with a 30-minute reserve?
questions.context: Referenced question 1: Winds are forecast to be 330° at 17 knots. You are planning a flight along a true course of 205° at 112 KTAS. Variation in the area is 16° W and your compass correction is +4°. What magnetic heading should you fly?

questions.question: During planning for a VFR cross-country flight, you determine that the first leg of your route is 112 nm. You calculate a ground speed of 243 km/hr. You'll lean for Best Economy and average 9.7 gph. How many gallons of fuel will be required for this leg including a 30-minute reserve?

What is the required fuel load for the following flight using a 30-minute day VFR reserve? [[NEXT_LINE]] TC=137°. [[NEXT_LINE]] Distance=349 km. [[NEXT_LINE]] TAS=151 kts. [[NEXT_LINE]] Winds aloft=080° at 37 kts. [[NEXT_LINE]] Fuel burn=9.7 GPH.

On a two engine jet at Mach 0.77 fuel flow is 1,800 lbs/hr per engine. At Mach 0.80 fuel flow is increased by 7% and at Mach 0.82 fuel flow is increased by 17%. It's a standard day at FL360 with calm winds and you are planning a 500 nm cruise leg at Mach 0.77. After 120 nm you increase speed to Mach 0.80 for 150 nm. You then decide to increase to Mach 0.82 for the remainder of the cruise. How much additional gas is consumed by flying faster?
How many gallons of Avgas are burned after 3 hours 27 minutes at an average rate of 76 pounds per hour?

Your cross-country flight is 104 nm long on a true course of 043°. You have already planned the flight, but your passenger wants to make a detour to see a famous landmark. Your new route will be a 358° course for 66 nm, then a 082° course for 75 nm. You will fly at 124 KTAS burning 11.2 GPH, and winds aloft are 110° at 42 kts. How much extra fuel do you need due to the course change?

In Loreta, Mexico the fuel truck pumps fuel at 19.5 liters/second. If your dispatch release indicates that you need 18,700 pounds of fuel total in your B737 and you landed with 5,430 pounds, how much fuel do you need to tell the fueler to fill up your jet with assuming Jet-A weighs 6.75 lbs/gallon?

Your engine is getting close to needing an overhaul, you've noticed your fuel consumption is about 7% higher than the POH value of 9.2 gph. If the repair shop is 173 nautical miles away, how much fuel will you burn flying your plane to the shop for a new engine? Your average groundspeed is 103 knots.
How many gallons of fuel does an aircraft burn during a flight of 3 hours 37 minutes if it averages 8.4 GPH?

In your aircraft, you can cruise at 154 mph TAS while burning 13.1 gph, or 133 mph TAS while burning 8.9 gph. Your true course today is 338°, winds aloft are 036@29 kts, and you have 34 gallons of fuel. How much additional range do you gain by flying at the slower speed assuming no reserve fuel?
Your aircraft cruises at 114 KTAS, and can carry 4 people. Disregarding wind, what is your per passenger fuel economy if you burn 9.2 gallons per hour of fuel?

The winds at your cruising altitude are forecasted to be 135° at 42 kts. You are flying a true course of 335°. Your destination is 486 nm from your departure point. How many gallons of fuel will you burn and what true heading will you fly on this flight? Your Cruise Master 100 burns 110.4 pph of 100LL gasoline and averages a TAS of 192 mph.

Your cross country flight is 278 sm long on a true course of 172°. You will cruise at 173 KTAS while burning 12.3 GPH. Winds aloft are 220° at 28 knots. What is your minimum takeoff fuel to maintain a 30-minute VFR reserve?

The fuel truck pumps 220 gallons of JET-A (6.8 lbs/gal) per minute. Each of your wing tanks holds 14,000 lbs, and the center tank holds 10,500 lbs. If you landed with 6,000 lbs total, how long will it take to top off all the tanks?
You have just completed a 237 NM cross country at an average ground speed of 106 KTS. If you burn 9.3 GPH how much total fuel was burned?
Reference the previous question. Fuel costs $1.79/liter. How much is it to refuel?
questions.context: Referenced question 15: You have just completed a 237 NM cross country at an average ground speed of 106 KTS. If you burn 9.3 GPH how much total fuel was burned?

questions.question: Referencing the previous question, if the aircraft cost $127 per hour without fuel, how much money will you spend on this flight if the fuel cost $4.37/GAL?
questions.context: Referenced question 9: You need 1.8 hours of PIC time before you can take your commercial checkride. Your Piper burns 9.4 GPH with a tank of 42 gallons. How far out do you need to travel at 138 KTAS with winds 245° at 38 KTS on a course of 296° to get your required time?

questions.question: After flying for 3:24:00 your aircraft has burned 141.4 liters. How many liters are you burning per hour?
Your flight covers 480 NM in four hours at a ground speed of 120 KTS. If your average fuel burn is 9.5 GPH, how many total liters will you burn?

You are planning a 107 SM cross-country flight on a true course of 072°. The winds are 030° at 27 knots, and you will cruise at 118 KTAS. If you have 24 gallons of fuel on board, use 2.2 gallons for taxi/takeoff, and burn 9.4 gallons per hour, how much fuel endurance will you have remaining at your destination?
The Cherokee you rented has a usable fuel capacity of 36 U.S. gallons. How far can you fly in still air, landing with FAA VFR day fuel reserves? You lean to burn 15.785 kg/hour and cruise at 115 mph.

You are planning a short trip in your vintage plane. Your route is 48 NM on a true course of 317°. True winds aloft are 150° at 18 KTS at your cruising altitude of 3,500' (ISA). Your plane cruises at 98 mph indicated while sipping only 5.5 gallons per hour of fuel. If you top off the single 42 liter tank, how much reserve fuel will you land with?

You just replaced your eight-cylinder avgas-guzzling engine with a new, 280 kW electric motor. The airplane's new endurance time aloft is 2:45. Considering this, how much avgas burn does this system replace if the manual states that 33.7 kWh equals one gallon of avgas? Hint: 1 kWh = 1 kW of power used in one hour

Referencing the previous question, you originally planned for the winds to be 317° at 27 knots. Your flight is 172 NM. With these new winds, how much longer or shorter do you recalculate your flight to be? How much less or more fuel will you burn at 12.8 GPH?
questions.context: Referenced question 39: Ack!! It turns out that the winds aloft are not as forecast. You are attempting to maintain a true course of 330°. Flying a true heading of 337° results in drifting four degrees right of course. Flying a true heading of 322° results in drifting two degrees left of course. Your true airspeed is 147 knots. What are the actual winds aloft?

questions.question: You just got your brand new all electric airplane and you want to try it out on a short cross country flight. Your flight is 63 NM on a true course of 216°, and winds aloft are 170° at 32 KTS. Your new airplane cruises at 93 KTAS while consuming 72 Amp-hours (Ah) of power per hour. If the aircraft is equipped with a 250-Ah battery, what is the minimum charge level for departure if you want to land with your legal VFR reserve of 30 minutes?

You're the captain of a Boeing 777 preparing to fly from Hong Kong to Memphis, TN. Your planned fuel burn for this flight is 173,000 pounds of Jet-A. While the aircraft is being loaded, aircraft maintenance notifies you that they've had to remove 4 feet of a rubber seal from your flaps. Per the aircraft manual you're required to apply a 0.09% over burn for each foot of missing seal. At the same time, you get notification from the ramp crew that you will be carrying dry ice in both your forward and aft belly cargo compartments. Per the aircraft manual, carriage of dry ice requires a 0.4% over burn for each belly compartment in which the dry ice is located. How much additional fuel do you need to put on the aircraft, knowing that in Hong Kong, the fuel measurement is in liters? (Assume fuel weight of 6.7 pounds/gallon.)

In cruise the Boeing 737 burns 2,916 LBS/HR per engine, the Challenger 300 burns 880 LBS/HR per engine. If both aircraft cruise at 0.80 Mach (-50°C). How much more efficient is the Challenger than the 737?
You wish to visit a friend for dinner. The flight will be 233 NM at a ground speed of 94 KTS at 7.4 GPH fuel burn. How much fuel will you need to carry for this flight including day VFR reserves?

The fuel truck in Aruba dispenses Jet-A at the rate of 13.2 liters/second. If your dispatch requested fuel load is 34,900 pounds, and you currently have 8,430 pounds of fuel on board, how long will it take to pump the required fuel? Assume fuel density of 6.7 pounds/gallon.

At FL130 your aircraft burns 15,415 pounds/hour at 320 KIAS. At FL180 it burns 16,262 pounds/hour at 320 KIAS. Assuming a linear change and a fuel density of 6.77 pounds/gallon, how many gallons of JET-A will you burn at FL150 at 320 KIAS?

At high power cruise you will burn 17 GPH and cruise at 167 knots. If you pull the power back to an economy cruise fuel flow will drop to 14 GPH and your speed will fall to 151 knots. Over a 400-NM trip how much fuel will you save?

You plan a 147 NM-flight using winds aloft of 030° at 28 KTS and true course of 349°. At max cruise power, your aircraft cruises at 139 KTAS and burns 10.2 GPH; at economy cruise it goes 122 KTAS and burns 7.7 GPH. If 100LL is $4.87 per gallon at your home airport, how much money do you save by cruising at economy power instead of max power?

You will be taking a short flight today from your home airport to a nearby airport for lunch. The true course to your destination is 322° and the distance is 133 NM. If you plan to fly at 105 KIAS at 7,500 feet on a standard day where the winds are 033° true at 39 knots, how much fuel will you need to meet day VFR reserves for this flight if you consume 11.2 gph of fuel?
Your airplane cruises at 197 KTAS while burning 29 GPH. If your truck can go 207 miles on one 25 gallon tank of gas, which one is more fuel efficient, and by how much?

You are flying a true course of 239, and the winds aloft are 260@28 kts. After leveling off, you lean the mixture according to the max power procedure and find that you cruise at 148 KTAS while burning 10.3 GPH. You decide to try the lean-of-peak max range procedure and find that you are now cruising at 135 KTAS while burning 7.4 GPH. How much range have you gained if you have 29 gallons left in the tanks?

After a long flight with strong headwinds, it looks like you will be landing with your minimum 45 minute reserve, planned at your cruise fuel burn of 19 GPH. After traffic forces you to execute a go around, you realize that your flaps will not retract, and you are burning a frightening 26.5 GPH. How long will your reserve fuel last you now?
How fast is an aircraft flying if it burned 512 fluid ounces of avgas at a rate of 42.4 liters per hour over 86 statute miles?
What is the minimum amount of AVGAS needed to fly 590 km at 185 knots including 0:45 minutes of reserve? Use an average fuel burn of 12.6 GPH.
If the cost of 100LL is $5.20 per gallon, how much will it cost to refuel your aircraft after burning 15 gallons on a cross country flight?
On your most recent cross country, you covered 452 statute miles, while burning 170 liters of fuel at an average ground speed of 196 kilometers per hour. What was your average fuel burn?
How much avgas was burned after an aircraft flew 385 kilometers at 177 miles per hour? The average rate of burn was 0.88 liters per minute.
Your airplane burns 300 LBS/HR. of Jet-A (6.8 LBS/GAL.). You are going on a 1 hour 37 minute flight, and you want to carry a 45 minute reserve. How many liters of Jet-A do you need on board?
Your airplane can hold 431 pounds of usable avgas and burns 53.75 L/hr. What is your endurance if you plan to land with the required 30-minute day/VFR reserve?
If you fly 245 nm at 125 kts ground speed while burning 9.7 gph how much fuel have you burned?

A KC-135 Stratotanker weighing 268,000 lbs is transferring JP-8 (specific gravity of 6.5 lbs/gal) to a B-52 at a rate of 1,763 L/min. Each of the KC-135's four engines burns 3,200 lb/h. If the B-52 needs to take on 50,000 lbs of gas, how much time does the fuel transfer take?
Referring to the previous question: Including the fuel transfer and the fuel burn from the KC-135's engines, how much fuel drains from the KC-135's fuel tanks during the process?
questions.context: Referenced question 13: A KC-135 Stratotanker weighing 268,000 lbs is transferring JP-8 (specific gravity of 6.5 lbs/gal) to a B-52 at a rate of 1,763 L/min. Each of the KC-135's four engines burns 3,200 lb/h. If the B-52 needs to take on 50,000 lbs of gas, how much time does the fuel transfer take?

questions.question: You depart on a quick scenic flight with full tanks and burn 850 lbs/hr. for 47 minutes. How many gallons of Jet-A will it take to top off the tanks when you land (assuming standard weight of Jet A)?

You are cruising at FL370 (ISA+4) at M0.70 and burning 1100 lbs/hr of Jet-A on each of your two engines. The FMS shows a headwind component of 34 kts. You are considering accelerating to M0.78, which would increase your fuel burn to 1400 lbs/hr per engine. How much extra fuel would you burn at M0.78 if there is 286 nm left in the flight? Disregard descent for these calculations.
On your most recent cross country, you covered 226 statute miles, while burning 85 liters of fuel at an average ground speed of 204 kilometers per hour. What was your average fuel burn?
Quinque plans to burn 5.67 GPH in cruise. However, after 4500 seconds in flight, he determines he has actually burned 31.42 liters. How much fuel will he burn after an additional 1 HR 45 MINS?

Imagine that the fuel quantity gauges are showing a total of 50 GALS on board, and the "total fuel used" gauge is showing 27.2 GALS used over the past 2 HRS and 17 MINS. At departure, you had 86 GALS on board. You have a fuel leak between the tank and the fuel flow sensor that is causing the difference, and you want to know if you'll have enough fuel to get to the more operationally favorable diversion airport, rather than the closest in point of time. To get to the operationally favorable diversion airport you will burn 13 GALS cruise, descent, and landing, and those steps will require 48 MINS. You'd like to land with at least 6 GALS on board as a safe reserve. If you assume that the leak rate has been constant since takeoff, and divert now, how much fuel will you have on landing at the operationally favorable diversion airport?
If your 265 NM cross country took 106 MINS, and your planned fuel burn was 13.2 GPH, how much fuel did you plan on using?
How far has an aircraft traveled after burning 15.3 gallons of fuel from their 32-gallon capacity at an average burn of 10.7 GPH and average speed of 150 MPH?
How much fuel will he burn in total?
questions.context: Use the following information for questions 11-15: Joey plans a flight from KMRN (1270') to KTYS (979') at 8,500' MSL on a true course of 277°. The total distance is 118 NM and he will cruise at 145 MPH TAS and 8.7 GPH with winds aloft 240° at 14 KTS. His average climb performance will be 650 FPM at 92 KTAS and 9.8 GPH with average winds of 215° at 15 KTS. His average descent performance will be 900 FPM at 110 KTAS and 5.2 GPH with average winds of 320° at 8 KTS.

questions.question: You expect a fuel burn of 8.2 GPH. After 3 hours and 17 minutes you find that you have burned 29.2 GALS. Which answer choice best describes your fuel situation?

choices.text: You've burned 2.3 gallons more than expected.
You've actually been burning 9.2 gallons per hour.
Your planned burn rate matches your actual rate within .2 gph.
You've burned 2.9 gallons less than expected.

questions.correctChoice: You've burned 2.3 gallons more than expected.

questions.question: A 757 (two-engine airliner) has its 11,480 gallon tanks completely filled early in the cool morning when the Jet A density is 6.74 lbs./gal. The aircraft is delayed and the rising temperature causes the Jet A to expand and subsequently leak out the fuel vents. At its peak temperature, the Jet A had a density of 6.62 lbs./gal. How much fuel was lost through the vents?
Referencing the above question, what is the resulting loss of endurance if the aircraft planned on burning 1550 lbs./hr./engine, in cruise?
questions.context: Referenced question 1: A 757 (two-engine airliner) has its 11,480 gallon tanks completely filled early in the cool morning when the Jet A density is 6.74 lbs./gal. The aircraft is delayed and the rising temperature causes the Jet A to expand and subsequently leak out the fuel vents. At its peak temperature, the Jet A had a density of 6.62 lbs./gal. How much fuel was lost through the vents?

questions.question: Every few months you fly your single engine plane loaded with Girl Scout cookies out to a secluded island. It is 192 sm away on a 264° course. How much more fuel is burned flying out to the island compared to the return flight back? Each flight you burn 3 gallons climbing and descending. All climbs and descents happen over the airport. Winds aloft are 300@29 knots and flying at 142 KTAS your cruise fuel burn is 8.8 GPH.

You're planning a trip from Page Field, Fort Myers (FMY) to Panama City, FL (ECP) in your Grumman Tiger. You're bringing a lot of bags so you want to maximize payload by carrying only the total burn plus a 45 min reserve. How much fuel do you depart with?
questions.context: Use the following information to answer questions 24-26. [[NEXT_LINE]] TC = 334°, Distance = 394 SM, TAS = 124 knots, True Winds = 229°@27 knots, Fuel Flow = 6.8 gal/hr. Cruise altitude = 8,000' MSL

questions.question: A trailer-mounted auxiliary fueling device holds 100 gallons of AVGAS in a horizontally-oriented cylindrical tank that measures 22 inches in diameter. Fuel is dispensed by a 12-volt electric pump at the rate of 12 gallons per minute. What is the quantity of the fuel remaining in the tank after the pump has been running for 3.2 minutes?

After installing a fuel flow meter, you learn to lean your engine based on indicated AVGAS fuel flow. Before leaning, your engine is set for cruise power and is burning 9.5 gallons per hour. After leaning, it is burning 7.8 gallons per hour. How much fuel does the leaned condition save over 1 hour and 46 minutes in cruise?

It's day one of your new airline job, and shortly after takeoff you realize you left your mocha frappuccino at the gate. The captain agrees this is worth returning to the airport. Before you land you need to reduce your weight to max landing weight (74,957 LBS.). From where you start your approach it will take 12 minutes to land. Your burn rate is 3600 LBS/HR (assume that it stays constant). How long before you start your return to the airport to touch down at exactly max landing weight and retrieve your yummy beverage? Your current weight is 78,032 LBS.

When you saw your cousins while taxiing in, you noticed a fourth person waiting for you. Uh, oh! It turns out it is Lester, the evil spawn child of your aunt and uncle. You remember you still have 15 gallons of fuel left and you don't want to take Lester on the trip with your three good cousins. It's a calm day and you know that your Cherokee burns fuel at 12.2 gal/hr. cruising at 182 KTS. How long could you take only Lester flying and safely return to the airport with day VFR fuel reserves?
questions.context: Use the following information for questions 25-28. [[DIVIDER]] Referenced question 25: It is a beautiful afternoon and you decide you are going to fly from Cherokee, Iowa to Des Moines, Iowa (TC = 132°) to pick up your three cousins and fly them around to see some farmland. Billy Jo weighs 45.3 kilograms, Sally Sue weighs 54.4 kilograms, and Cletus weighs 81.8 kilograms. For weight and balance purposes, how many pounds combined do your three cousins weigh?

questions.question: If you wanted to fly straight on the outbound heading until you ran out of fuel, how far could you travel?
questions.context: Use the following data for questions 11-14: [[NEXT_LINE]] TC: 170 outbound Pressure altitude: 5,000' IAS: 140 knots. [[NEXT_LINE]] Winds: 200 @ 25 knots OAT: 0 C Fuel: 40 US gal Burn: 9.5 gph.

questions.question: Your friend turns out to be alright though, and you arrive in GRB without incident. If fuel costs $5.74 per gallon, how much did you spend on fuel for your flight today?
questions.context: Use the following info for questions 30-32: [[NEXT_LINE]] Dist. = 110 nm Magnetic Course (GRB-TVC) = 083 TAS = 121 mph [[NEXT_LINE]] Magnetic Winds = 310@35 knots Fuel Flow = 5.5 gph [[NEXT_LINE]] Assume the same performance for climb and descent.

questions.question: Refer to the previous problem. You want to empty your tip tanks into your main tanks as quickly as you can, but your main tanks are completely full so you must burn off enough fuel first. It takes 30 minutes from the time you start the transfer for the tip tanks to be empty and you are burning 17.5 gph. How long after startup do you have to fly before starting the transfer if you want your mains to be completely full the instant the transfer is complete and the tips are empty? Add 1.5 gallons of fuel burn for taxi/takeoff.
questions.context: Referenced question 6: You are ready to fly your aircraft with a weight of 3,650 lbs and a moment of 155,125 in-lbs, when you decide to fill your tip tanks with 100LL which hold 15 gallons per side at station 48. What will your new CG be?

questions.question: The specific range of an aircraft can be expressed as nautical miles per gallon (NM/GAL). What is the specific range for an aircraft after flying 3:15:00 at an average ground speed of 128 knots? Average fuel burn is 7.2 GPH.

Your crazy Uncle Bernie is building his own aircraft. The fuel system is a bit complicated though. It is made of one 55 gallon drum, six 4.5L carboys, and a container that used to hold 17.8 imperial gallons of imported hot pepper relish. What is the estimated range in still air if the aircraft will burn 12.6 gph and cruise at 144 knots? Include the required FAA minimum 30 minute reserve.

During preflight, you notice a small oil leak. You estimate that it is leaking at 2.7 pph. Before takeoff, you top off the oil at 6 quarts. The aircraft is fueled with 77 usable gallons of 100LL aviation gasoline. Your preflight planning indicates that you will burn 107.1 pph for the flight. Will you run out of gas or oil first? How much remaining fluid will be left once the other is exhausted?
A gas turbine engine burns 1600 pounds per hour of jet fuel. How many gallons have gone to the engine after 5 seconds? Assume one gallon equals 6.8 pounds.
For every 4 hours of flying time, your old biplane burns a quart of oil. How many gallons must you add back to the engine after flying 272 nm at 97 KTAS. Wind Calm.
How much fuel is used outbound at 8,500' MSL?
questions.context: Use the following information to answer questions 11 through 15: [[TABLE]]

questions.question: How much fuel will be needed for a 318 NM flight if you want to land with 30 minutes of reserve fuel? Your aircraft cruises at 122 knots TAS while burning 9.2 gallons per hour. Your TC is 157° and the wind is 341° at 37 knots.

choices.text: 18.6 Imperial gallons Standard temperature and pressure Variation: 7W Magnetic heading: 159° Total distance: 206 statute miles Total fuel burn: 14.48 US gallons Departure elevation: 1,350' Destination elevation: 820' Climb: 10m15s and 2.27 US gallons of fuel 84 knot ground speed. Cruise: 127.5 knot ground speed Descent: 8m21s and 0.86 US gallons of fuel 800 fpm descent rate. 122 knot ground speed

questions.question: You set out to build some flight time and explore in a new Skycatcher. It has a total fuel capacity of 25.46 gallons. Each wing tank holds 0.73 gallons of unusable fuel. You plan to fly outbound from your home airport on a true course of 260° at 4,500'. The winds at 3,000' are 080@25 knots and at 6,000' are 130@7 knots. If you lean for an economy cruise, the Skycatcher will sip fuel at an incredible 3.7 gph and maintain a blistering 78 KTAS. How long can you fly until needing fuel if you wish to land with a 30 min reserve?
Referring to the Skycatcher question above, how far away will you be when you need fuel?
questions.context: Referenced question 30: You set out to build some flight time and explore in a new Skycatcher. It has a total fuel capacity of 25.46 gallons. Each wing tank holds 0.73 gallons of unusable fuel. You plan to fly outbound from your home airport on a true course of 260° at 4,500'. The winds at 3,000' are 080@25 knots and at 6,000' are 130@7 knots. If you lean for an economy cruise, the Skycatcher will sip fuel at an incredible 3.7 gph and maintain a blistering 78 KTAS. How long can you fly until needing fuel if you wish to land with a 30 min reserve?

questions.question: Given: [[NEXT_LINE]] Winds aloft: 203° @ 23 knots [[NEXT_LINE]] WCA: 10° R [[NEXT_LINE]] TAS: 121 knots [[NEXT_LINE]] MH: 135° [[NEXT_LINE]] Variation: 7° W [[NEXT_LINE]] Distance flown: 211 sm [[NEXT_LINE]] Fuel flow: 8.4 gph [[NEXT_LINE]] Deviation: -4° [[NEXT_LINE]] How much gas was used for this flight?
If you burned 92.1 Imperial gallons of fuel on a 6 hour and 32 min flight, what was your burn rate in U.S. gallons?
He carried 451 gallons of fuel, and landed with 85 gallons. What was his average fuel burn?

Fast-forward to 1977 when the crew of an Air France Concorde flew the inaugural trip between New York and Paris. Those smart alecks were really putting old Lindy to shame by cruising at Mach 2.02. If they flew 3650 statute miles in 3 hours 35 minutes and burned 46 pounds per mile, what was the fuel burn rate?
Assuming a jet fuel weight of 6.8 pounds per gallon and an aircraft capacity of 98 passengers, what is the fuel economy in the previous question?
questions.context: Referenced question 29: Fast-forward to 1977 when the crew of an Air France Concorde flew the inaugural trip between New York and Paris. Those smart alecks were really putting old Lindy to shame by cruising at Mach 2.02. If they flew 3650 statute miles in 3 hours 35 minutes and burned 46 pounds per mile, what was the fuel burn rate?

questions.question: Referring back to the Lindberg questions, and realizing that the Spirit of St. Louis only has one seat, what was the fuel economy in miles per gallon of Lindberg's flight?
questions.context: Referenced question 23: When Charles Lindberg crossed the Atlantic Ocean in 1927, he flew for 33.5 hours and traveled 3610 statute miles. What was his average speed? [[DIVIDER]] Referenced question 25: He carried 451 gallons of fuel, and landed with 85 gallons. What was his average fuel burn?

questions.question: You're traveling at 317 km/hr. If you've flown 502 km and burned 82.7 lbs./min, what is your total fuel burn?

choices.text: 7858 lbs BEW 2651 lbs. 264.32 Front Pas. 294 lbs. 59" () Rear Pas. () 84" 18.17 Bags 59 lbs. () 8.16 Fuel (84 Gals) 72" ()

questions.correctChoice: 7858 lbs BEW 2651 lbs. 264.32 Front Pas. 294 lbs. 59" () Rear Pas. () 84" 18.17 Bags 59 lbs. () 8.16 Fuel (84 Gals) 72" ()

questions.question: You fly between two airports, a total distance of 324 sm. The wind is 173°/23 kts and a TC of 148°. Your average fuel burn is 12.3 gal/hr and your TAS is 102 kts. What is the fuel required for this trip (US gals)?
Your aircraft burns 61.4 pph. With 43 gallons on board, how many hours can you fly?
If an aircraft burns 4.2 gallons in 28 minutes, how much will it burn per hour?
An aircraft burns 4.9 gallons in 17 minutes and 22 seconds. How much will it burn in 2 hours and 37 minutes?
The total fuel consumed is
questions.context: Use the following to answer questions 27 - 31. You're departing Hometown airport (elevation 2280') with a 400 fpm rate of climb to a cruise altitude of 6500 feet. Your groundspeed in the climb is 85 kts and in cruise it is 120 kts. You descend to 1000 feet above destination airport (elevation 1700) with a groundspeed of 140 kts and a de scent rate of 650 fpm. Your fuel consumption is 11 gph in climb, 9.5 gph in cruise and 7 gph in descent. You are carrying 27 gallons of usable fuel. The total distance of the flight is 295 nm.

questions.question: Would you be able to make this flight and still have a 30 minute reserve?
questions.context: Use the following to answer questions 27 - 31. You're departing Hometown airport (elevation 2280') with a 400 fpm rate of climb to a cruise altitude of 6500 feet. Your groundspeed in the climb is 85 kts and in cruise it is 120 kts. You descend to 1000 feet above destination airport (elevation 1700) with a groundspeed of 140 kts and a de scent rate of 650 fpm. Your fuel consumption is 11 gph in climb, 9.5 gph in cruise and 7 gph in descent. You are carrying 27 gallons of usable fuel. The total distance of the flight is 295 nm.

questions.question: How much fuel will you burn in 3:22:00 at 12.1 GPH?
84.1 Imperial gallons of fuel burned over a 5.43 hr flight. What was the burn rate (US)?

You began a trip with full tanks. You've traveled for 2:40:00, and used 21.7 gallons of fuel. With 1:22:00 of flying time remaining, and a ground speed of 141 kts. how much fuel will it take to fill the tanks at the end of the flight-.

How much fuel will be needed for a flight based on the following data? Given: [[NEXT_LINE]] Fuel consumption: 57.2 pph [[NEXT_LINE]] Wind: 315@14 kts. [[NEXT_LINE]] True course: 132° Trip length: 412 NM [[NEXT_LINE]] Indicated airspeed: 126 kts. [[NEXT_LINE]] Indicated altitude: 6,500' (pressure altitude is the same) [[NEXT_LINE]] Outside air temperature: + 16°C
Your aircraft is filled with 41 gallons of useable fuel. At 9.65 gallons per hour, how much fuel (useable) will be left if you fly 323 NM in 117 minutes?
If an aircraft uses 38.1 gallons of fuel at 10.7 gallons per hour while flying 518 NM, what is its groundspeed?
How much will remain upon landing?
questions.context: Referenced question 24: The distance between two checkpoints Is 18 NM. You cross the first checkpoint at 10:11. At 10:17 you cross checkpoint #2. The remaining trip distance is 175 NM. What will be your ETA based on your computed groundspeed? [[DIVIDER]] Refer to the previous question to answer t he following: You've traveled 87 NM upon crossing checkpoint #2 and used 9.1 gallons of fuel. You departed with 65 gallons of useable fuel.

questions.question: Your aircraft travels 13.1 NM per gallon of fuel. At a true airspeed of 206 knots, how many gallons per hour does it burn? (no wind)

An aircraft flies to a destination 297 NM away. at a true airspeed of 109 kts. With 48.5 gallons of useable fuel, at an average rate of 11.5 gph, how long can the aircraft stay aloft over the destination airport and still have 30 minutes of fuel left? (Assume NO WIND)
If the fuel burn is 11.5 gallons per hour, how much AVGAS will you burn in 1 hour and 54 minutes?
If AVGAS consumption is 15 pounds per hour, what is the fuel burn for 1 hour and 14 minutes?
You plan a flight of 4:00 hours. Your plane uses 8.1 gallons of fuel per hour. How many gallons are required for the trip with a:30 fuel reserve?
Your aircraft burns.8 gallons of fuel in 9 minutes. What is the hourly fuel burn rate?
If you fly 402 nm, with a fuel burn rate of 12.5 gph, and use 35 gallons, what is your ground speed?
You are planning a 296 sm flight; the PA is 5,000'; wind is calm; OAT is -1°C; IAS will be 129 mph and the fuel burn rate is 9.3 gph. How many gallons of fuel are required (no reserve)?
If your aircraft burns 32.2 gallons of fuel at the rate of 9.7 gph while flying 385 miles; what is the aircraft's speed?

How much fuel will be burned during the following flight? PA = 9,500', Temp = standard for that altitude, [[NEXT_LINE]] IAS = 153 mph, [[NEXT_LINE]] Wind = 044° at 26 knots, [[NEXT_LINE]] TC = 315°, [[NEXT_LINE]] Distance = 371 statute miles, [[NEXT_LINE]] Fuel Burn Rate = 63 lbs. per hour
How much fuel will you burn in 2:34:00 at 11.3 GPH?

You began a trip with full tanks. So far you've traveled for 2:32:00 at a ground speed of 118 mph and used 27 gallons of fuel. With 1:27:00 of flying time, 216 sm remaining and a ground speed of 127 kts., how much fuel will it take to fill the tanks at the end of the flight-.
How much fuel (US gals) will be needed for a flight based on the following data?
If you fly 376 sm, with a fuel burn rate of 9.7 gph, and use 29.7 gallons, what is your ground speed?
You are planning a 562 sm flight, the PA is 15,000', wind is calm, OAT is -27°C, IAS will be 212 mph and the fuel burn rate is 92 lbs/hr. How many gallons of fuel is required (no reserve)?
How much fuel will be burned during the following flight?

choices.text: 335.3 US gal Temp = standard for that altitude
360.0 US gal Wind = 320 at 42 knots TC = 045 Distance = 721 statute miles Fuel Burn Rate = 751 pph

questions.question: Your aircraft is filled with 46 gallons of useable fuel. At 12.15 gallons per hour, how much fuel (useable) will be left if you fly 385 NM in 103 minutes?
If an aircraft uses 29.5 gallons of fuel at 10.8 gallons per hour while flying 521 NM, what is its groundspeed?
How much will remain upon landing?
questions.context: Referenced question 24: The distance between two cities is 374 SM and you cover it in 1:23:42. What must your TAS be under the following conditions: [[DIVIDER]] Refer to the previous question to answer the following: You've traveled 91 NM and used 8.7 gallons of fuel. You departed with 66 gallons of useable fuel.

questions.question: Your aircraft travels 12.9 NM per gallon of fuel. At a true airspeed of 207 kts, how many gallons per hour does it burn? (no wind)

An aircraft flies to a destination 387 NM away at a true airspeed of 131 kts. With 49.6 gallons of useable fuel, at an average rate of 13.7 gph, how long can the aircraft stay aloft over the destination airport and still have 30 minutes of fuel left? (Assume NO WIND) ____________________________________ (min: sec) Write your answer on the answer sheet
When you made the turn towards Salt Lake, you had 19700 pounds of fuel on board. How much fuel will you have upon arrival in Salt Lake City assuming that you burn an average of 9000 pounds per hour?

According to your dispatch release, you'll need to bring the fuel load up to 37000 pounds for your next leg from Salt Lake City to Memphis. Knowing that the fuel gauges on the exterior refueling panel are deferred for maintenance, you figure that you need to tell the fuel truck driver to uplift how many gallons of Jet-A (6.7 pounds/gallon) to bring you up to your desired fuel load?
How many gallons of fuel will you burn getting to that next checkpoint if you are burning 8.7 gal/hr?
You have already flown 345 nm at a speed of 110 kts. You started the trip with 40 gallons of fuel. You now have only 9 gallons left. How fast have you been burning fuel?
You are burning 49.2 liters of fuel per hour. You're flying at a speed of 172.6 mph and cover a distance of 185 km. How much fuel do you burn?
How many gallons of fuel will you burn for this flight? (Write your answer on the answer sheet to the nearest 1/10th of a gallon.)

You are 136 NM due east of the EDY VORTAC. You want to fly over Uncle Buck's house which is 83 km from EDY on the 270° radial. If you want to burn off exactly 28 liters of fuel enroute and your aircraft burns 5.6 Imperial gallons per hour, at what indicated airspeed should you fly? Winds are 120@17 kts (m agnetic), OAT is 68°F and pressure altitude is 6700 ft.
If average fuel burn was 1.6 liters per minute (lpm), how much fuel should be on board upon reaching FL270?
questions.context: Referenced question 34: You are planning your climb profile. From the airport (elevation 1300 ft.) you fly out on a TC of 040°. You fly 105 KTAS & 1800 fpm to 11,000 ft. Then you fly 125 KTAS & 1100 fpm to FL180. Upon reaching FL180 you turn to a TC of 120° and continue to climb at 140 KTAS & 600 fpm to FL270. Average winds are 060@18 knots. How long will it take to reach FL270? [[DIVIDER]] (Refer to previous problem) You departed with 73 gal of fuel on board.

questions.question: An aircraft burns 10.4 gph. If it takes 17 seconds to travel.9 NM, then how much fuel would be consumed while flying 146 NM?
Using the above information, your first choice is 34,000 feet. What is the fuel burn, for the entire flight?
questions.context: NIKE is interviewing you for a position flying their Gulfstream V. They are asking you some hypothetical questions to gage your response. Some background information is that you have 13,000 lbs of fuel onboard. The interviewer says for this flight the GV burns fuel at 2000 lbs/hr (above 32,000 feet) and 2200 lbs/hr (below 32,000 feet). Flying at 0.78M with the following winds aloft: [[TABLE]] You are traveling 2120 miles from Hillsboro, OR (HIO) to Honolulu, HI (HNL). The True Course from HIO to HNL is 232°.

questions.question: Using the above information, your next selection is to look at 36,000 feet. What is the time en route and fuel burn, for the entire flight?

choices.text: Time in flight = 306 minutes and Fuel burn = 10250 lbs
Time in flight = 312 minutes and Fuel burn = 10440 lbs
Time in flight = 317 minutes and Fuel burn = 10620 lbs
Time in flight = 323 minutes and Fuel burn = 10790 lbs

questions.correctChoice: Time in flight = 323 minutes and Fuel burn = 10790 lbs

questions.question: You fly for 2.5 hours out over the ocean at 34,000 feet and have to return to Hillsboro at the same altitude. The new winds on the return will be 240° at 21 knots. How much fuel will you have upon return to HIO?

questions.question: You are planning a flight to Grandma's airport, which is 155 SM away. True course is 085°. True airspeed is 127 knots. Winds are 300@17 knots. If fuel burn is 8.6 gph, how much fuel will you burn flying to Grandma's? Use given data for all phases of flight.
Refer to the previous problem. If you continued to the grass strip, how much fuel did you burn after passing KDAY? Disregard descent performance changes.
questions.context: Referenced question 22: You are enroute to your grass strip at a cruising altitude of 12,500 ft. where the OAT is -20°C. CAS is 127 knots and the local altimeter setting is 29.42. Fuel on board is 33 gal burning at a rate of 12 gph. Winds are 290@17 knots. Just then you look down and notice the KDAY airport below you. You know KDAY is 110 NM from your grass strip. Assuming you hold a TC of 310°, how far from KDAY will you be when the time to continue to the grass strip equals the time to turn back and fly to KDAY?

questions.question: Without any calculations, you assume the fuel burn in Route 2 is the average over the entire leg. You also assume, for each climb and descent, the time and distance it takes are 20 minutes and 75 nautical miles, how much fuel is burned during Route 2? Zero wind.
questions.context: You are flying for a corporation that has many corporate aircraft. On two separate days in two separate aircraft (a G IV and King Air), you are asked to fly from Madison, WI (800 feet MSL) to Rapid City, SD (2200 feet MSL). You fly in a standard atmosphere unless otherwise stated. Route 1 is with the G IV and route 2 is with the King Air and are as follows: Route 1 MSN to RAP direct (602 nautical miles) [[NEXT_LINE]] True Course = 287° G IV burns 2000 pounds of Jet A per hour and travels at Mach 0.77 (-45°C) Route 2 Leg 1 (MSN to DSM), Leg 2 (DSM to RAP) 294 nautical miles and 422 nautical miles respectively [[NEXT_LINE]] True Course = Leg 1 (248°) and Leg 2 (322°) King Air burns 750 pounds of Jet A per hour and travels at 230 knots, true airspeed Des Moines, IA (600 feet MSL) is a landing airport.

questions.question: If the King Air burns 1250 pounds per hour in climb and the G IV burns 3200 pounds per hour in climb, which will burn more fuel during its climb(s)?

questions.question: If the aircraft burns 46.7 liters/hr and there is 52 Imperial Gallons on board, can the aircraft depart MKE, go to and over-fly AZO, return to MKE, and have one hour of reserve fuel?

choices.text: Yes, he'll have 22 minutes extra on top of the hour of reserve fuel
Yes, he'll have 37 minutes extra on top of the hour of reserve fuel
Yes, he'll have one hour extra on top of the hour of reserve fuel
No, he'll have to consume some of his reserve fuel

questions.correctChoice: Yes, he'll have 37 minutes extra on top of the hour of reserve fuel
questions.context: Use information from problems 1 and 2. [[DIVIDER]] Referenced question 1: Tim is flying from General Mitchell International (MKE) in Milwaukee, WI to Kalamazoo/Battle Creek Regional (AZO) in Battle Creek, MI a distance of 281 nautical miles. His indicated airspeed is 142 knots at 7500 ft pressure altitude, on a true course of 094°, with the winds aloft of 160° at 25 knots. If Tim's altimeter setting is 29.99" and the OAT is 15°C, what is Tim's groundspeed? [[DIVIDER]] Problem 2 setup: Tim decides to over-fly AZO and return to MKE. [[DIVIDER]] Referenced question 2: If the winds aloft switch to 115° at 15 knots, giving him a groundspeed of 325 km/hr, what is the total flight time for the entire trip?

questions.question: You are flying from Austin, TX to San Antonio, TX (213 nautical miles). You only want to burn 36 U.S. Gallons of Avgas to and from San Antonio. The fuel burn rates and groundspeed are as follows. [[TABLE]] Which altitude will give you the desired result?

choices.text: None of the altitudes will work

questions.question: If you burn 13,500 lbs/hr, how much fuel will you save by going direct?
questions.context: At 7:30 am, Tim is departing Dallas-Fort Worth (1000 ft MSL) for Portland, OR (0 ft MSL). His routing essentially takes him direct to Denver Intl. (DIA), 900 nautical miles, then direct PDX, an additional 1150 statute miles. ATC allows him the option to fly direct to PDX, 1400 nautical miles. The pressure everywhere is 29.92" Hg. Climb and descent winds are calm. Cruise winds aloft = 280@80 knots. His performance is as follows: [[NEXT_LINE]] Climb True Airspeed = 360 knots, [[NEXT_LINE]] Cruise Airspeed 0.79 Mach, and [[NEXT_LINE]] Descent True Airspeed = 470 knots. Temperature at FL390 is -35°C. True Course DFW to DIA is 340°, True Course DIA to PDX is 295°, and True Course DFW to PDX is 315°. Rate of Climb is 1000 fpm and Rate of Descent is 1500 fpm.
